wherein is briefly, fully, and faithfully handled the art of chirurgery in its four parts, with all the several diseases unto them belonging; their definitions, causes, signes, prognosticks, and cures, both general and particular. docs.title: What Next You Bastard My Early Years
docs.title_suggest: What Next You Bastard My Early Years
subject: No Schooling
Autobiography
Dying
Guina Pig for Medical Experiment
first_sentence:
1. My Early Years
The Hall Family was heading home from their Grandparent's place at Sackville Street, Greenslopes. My mother Myrtle was rying Ron, George and Mervyn were walking beside Mum. I was four years old but was too sick and weak to walk so I was in the pram with my Father who was in control of the pram. My pram was speeding up, my father had let go of the pram!
I could hear my Mother yelling behind me, "Stop the pram!" My father was too busy laughing. The footpath was coming up fast, the pram hit the concrete gutter, bucked and rolled over, then shot me out of the pram. Mum was there first. Dad was splitting his sices laughing. I did not utter a word. Luckily I was not injured.
text: What Next You Bastard My Early Years

1. My Early Years
The Hall Family was heading home from their Grandparent's place at Sackville Street, Greenslopes. My mother Myrtle was rying Ron, George and Mervyn were walking beside Mum. I was four years old but was too sick and weak to walk so I was in the pram with my Father who was in control of the pram. My pram was speeding up, my father had let go of the pram!
I could hear my Mother yelling behind me, "Stop the pram!" My father was too busy laughing. The footpath was coming up fast, the pram hit the concrete gutter, bucked and rolled over, then shot me out of the pram. Mum was there first. Dad was splitting his sices laughing. I did not utter a word. Luckily I was not injured.

docs.title: Three Short Stories
subject: Part true
Part Fiction
first_sentence:
For The Family
The tongue of the lock could be heard falling into the keeper as the old oak door shut with force.
Mary was alone. Her room was small and box like. A single chair that was made in the eighteenth century which needed repair was the only piece of furniture in it. Beside it was a small brown case containing her only possessions, the clothing which she wore ten years before when she first joined the convent and a special bottle of wine stamped with the Vatican seal. At the other end of the room was a weather beaten door, which led to freedom. Above her head hung a very dim light that did nothing to brighten the room, no natural sunlight found its way in here. Mary opened the case and took these belongings out and held them to her face, tears rolling freely as she sat down on the old chair. Wiping the tears away, her mind turned back the hands of time. She was an eight-year-old girl again when this had all started.
text:
For The Family
The tongue of the lock could be heard falling into the keeper as the old oak door shut with force.
Mary was alone. Her room was small and box like. A single chair that was made in the eighteenth century which needed repair was the only piece of furniture in it. Beside it was a small brown case containing her only possessions, the clothing which she wore ten years before when she first joined the convent and a special bottle of wine stamped with the Vatican seal. At the other end of the room was a weather beaten door, which led to freedom. Above her head hung a very dim light that did nothing to brighten the room, no natural sunlight found its way in here. Mary opened the case and took these belongings out and held them to her face, tears rolling freely as she sat down on the old chair. Wiping the tears away, her mind turned back the hands of time. She was an eight-year-old girl again when this had all started.
Black as the ace of Spade 1970

first_sentence: Over 75 years ago in a paper that appeared in the 1923 issue of the Journal of American Statistical Association (pp. 841-852) entitled "First and Second Laws of Error," the late professor and head of vital statistics at the Harvard School of Public Health, Edwin Bidwell Wilson (1879-1964)' concurs with economics professor W.L. Crum's conclusions expressed in a paper published in the same journal in March 1923, entitled "The Use of the Median in Determining Seasonal Variation" (pp. 607-614) that "a good many series of data from economic sources probably may be better treated by the median than by the mean."

docs.title: Annals of the persecution in Scotland from the restoration to the revolution. By James Aikman ..
docs.title_suggest: Annals of the persecution in Scotland from the restoration to the revolution. By James Aikman ..
first_sentence: THE first annunciation of the gospel in Eden to fallen man, was accompanied with an assurance of persecution:-" I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed: it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel."

EARLY in the year, a pretty large meeting, both of the indulged and unindulged ministers, had been held in Edinburgh, for the purpose of considering the religious state of the country, and for endeavouring to heal the differences which still subsisted among themselves respecting what should have been long before dismissed as vexatious-the conduct of those who had declared against the resolutions, and who still lay under the sentence of some of the church courts.

publisher: printed for L. Chapman at the Crown in Popes-head Ally
text: Wherein is shewed, the first shaking of the earth, seems to be meant the putting down of the late King and bishops: the later shaking, a change of the present government. And reasons given for both. Also how, and in what manner this later earth-shaking may be according to scripture-prophesie: namely, by that earth-quake, Rev. 11.13 which is likewise opened. Lastly, here are many reasons given, why it may be hoped that this present parliament will prove to be that earth-quake (or at least begin the work) whereby the good old cause will be revived, the just rights of the people restored, and the nation setled upon a lasting and durable foundation of truth and righteousness. By John Canne

text: set forth in the royall and politique power of England, the first intent that makes a king is the peoples consent, Fortesc. cap. 13, and it is the effluxe of blood from the heart to the head and lives before it the laws as sinewes unite all the members, and the head can no more change them then the head of a naturall body can alter the ligaments of all the members, the regall power is not righteous that is meerly imperial : whereunto is annexed six reasons to prove that it is no treason nor rebellion to obey the ordinances of Parliament against the personall commands of the King bee they never so royall or regall : with six reasons obligatory why the powers in Parliament are to be obeyed both by persons departed and all the people distracted, the first is indicium Regni.
